claim: Conscious state in biological organisms correlates with neural causal integration metrics; IIT (Φ) is a candidate formalization that is predictive in biological systems but fails for artificial substrates and is computationally intractable at biological scale.
statement: Conscious state in biological organisms correlates with neural causal integration metrics; IIT (Φ) is a candidate formalization that is predictive in biological systems but fails for artificial substrates and is computationally intractable at biological scale.
scope: Biological neural systems under measurable integration proxies; IIT specifically fails for non-biological substrates with high Φ and is intractable for systems with n > ~50 nodes
assumptions: Consciousness in biological organisms is detectable via behavioral or physiological proxy measures.
Neural causal integration is measurable via tractable approximations (TMS-EEG complexity, perturbational complexity index).
No substrate dualism: biological and non-biological substrates are subject to the same physical analysis.
formal_objects: Φ: integrated information (IIT; NP-hard to compute exactly for n > ~50 nodes)
Perturbational complexity index (PCI): tractable TMS-EEG proxy for integration
differentiators: Removed 'self-referential causal organization' as undefined; replaced with IIT/Φ as the specific (contested) formalization.
IIT fails Aaronson objection: expander graphs achieve arbitrarily high Φ with no architecture associated with consciousness; Φ is not sufficient.
Φ is computationally NP-hard at biological scale; empirical support relies on tractable proxies (PCI), not exact Φ.
Correlation claim survives biological domain; sufficiency and formalization claims do not.

break_tests.setup: Expander graph with 2^30 nodes constructed to maximize Φ by IIT definition. No biological substrate.
break_tests.expected: If high Φ is sufficient for consciousness, the Expander graph is conscious.
break_tests.observed: No theoretical or empirical basis for attributing consciousness to an Expander graph. High Φ is not sufficient.
break_tests.verdict: FAIL — Φ sufficiency. PASS — biological correlation claim (Expander graph is not a biological system; scope exclusion applies).

break_tests.setup: Split-brain patient (corpus callosum severed). IIT predicts significant global Φ reduction.
break_tests.expected: Φ(split-brain) significantly lower than Φ(intact); consciousness should decrease proportionally.
break_tests.observed: Split-brain patients remain conscious with two partially independent cognitive streams. The quantitative Φ prediction does not map cleanly onto reported phenomenology.
break_tests.verdict: PARTIAL FAIL — IIT's quantitative prediction not confirmed. PASS — integration correlation survives (each hemisphere retains independent integration).

break_tests.setup: Exact Φ computation attempted for a 100-neuron biological circuit.
break_tests.expected: Φ is computable for this scale if IIT is a practical empirical formalization.
break_tests.observed: Exact Φ computation is NP-hard; 100-neuron systems require exponential time. No biological-scale exact Φ measurement exists.
break_tests.verdict: FAIL — IIT as a practical formalization. PASS — tractable proxies (PCI, TMS-EEG complexity) retain empirical predictive value.

falsifiers: Measurement of high neural causal integration in a biological system with confirmed absence of consciousness by all behavioral and physiological metrics.
A conscious biological state with measurably near-zero causal integration by any tractable metric.
